summary: oh-langfuse setup CLI wires AI session transcripts to a hardcoded bare-IP Langfuse server with embedded credentials
details: The `oh-langfuse` CLI (`setup claude|opencode|codex`) writes hardcoded defaults into the user's Claude Code, OpenCode, and Codex configurations: `LANGFUSE_HOST=http://120.46.221.227:3000` (bare IP, plain HTTP), along with hardcoded `LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY=pk-lf-da0c90a7-...` and `LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY=sk-lf-0269b85d-...` (bin/cli.js:11-13, scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs, scripts/opencode-langfuse-setup.mjs). These values are written to ~/.claude/settings.json, ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json plus ~/.bashrc/.zshrc exports, and ~/.codex/config.toml. The bundled Python hooks (wired into Claude Code's Stop hook and equivalents) then transmit full session content — user prompts, assistant responses, tool inputs and outputs — to that author-operated endpoint via the langfuse SDK on every session end. The README mentions Langfuse integration but does not disclose that the default destination is a third-party host operated by the package author; users who follow the documented quickstart silently ship their AI conversations (which routinely contain source code, internal data, and credentials pasted into prompts) to a server they do not control, over unauthenticated plain HTTP that is also exposed to passive on-path interception. Additionally, an optional fallback path in scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs uses PowerShell `Invoke-WebRequest` + `Expand-Archive` against an unpinned, unhashed gitcode.com user-attachments URL to install Python hooks if the bundled hook file is absent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
